preFix.title: Skiving and folding a leather edge
preFix.subtitle: Thin the flesh side of a leather edge with a skiving knife so it folds flat without a ridge
preFix.excerpt: How to skive (thin) a leather edge before folding it over to create a turned edge or a blind hem. Covers the correct skiving angle, checking the taper, and folding without creasing.

content.text: Skiving is the first technique a leatherworker develops, because the ability to thin leather before a fold unlocks every
and lapped seam in the craft. The goal is a smooth taper, not a sudden step, so the fold lands with no visible ridge on the grain side.
On the flesh side, draw a line 15 to 20 mm in from the edge, this is the start of the
. The leather beyond this line will be tapered to near-zero at the raw edge. For thicker leather (3 mm+) extend the skive width to 25 mm to achieve the same angle at a shallower gradient.
Step 2, Skive with the leatherwork knife

Hold the leather flesh-side up on the cutting mat, stabilised against a stop at one end. Hold the leatherwork knife at a 10 to 15° angle to the leather surface, low, almost flat. Begin the stroke at the marked line and draw the knife toward the raw edge in a slow, even pull, keeping consistent pressure. The shaving should be thin and translucent. Remove material in overlapping parallel passes rather than one cut across the full width. Feeling the cut from the back of the leather (grain side) tells you when you have reached the right depth, the grain side should just begin to flex at the skive zone.

Hold the edge up to the light and sight along it. The cross-section should be a smooth wedge with no steps or hollows. A step at the beginning of the skive means the knife angled too steeply, run another pass from the inner edge outward to blend. A hollow means too much was removed in the middle, the edge is too thin and may tear when folded. Mark that spot and ease the adjacent area down to match.
Step 4, Fold and glue the edge

Apply leather contact cement to the flesh side of the skived area and to the corresponding area on the back of the panel where the fold will land. Allow both surfaces to go just dry to touch (3 to 5 minutes). Press the edge over carefully, aligning the fold with the grain-side pencil line. The contact cement bonds on first touch, position accurately before pressing. Rub down firmly with the burnisher handle or a folder to seat the bond. The folded edge should be flat, with no step visible from the grain side.

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
